text:
While the primary aim of all ‘omics’ sciences is to measure the entire complement of biological molecules, metabolomics is rather unique in that it is much more difficult to obtain a truly global view. The metabolome is made up of a chemically diverse class of small molecules with a wide range of physical properties, including extremes of polarity and hydrophobicity (from polar sugar phosphates to non-polar lipids) and a corresponding wide range of molecular masses. While many current metabolomics approaches introduce some degree of selectivity, there are generally two sources of bias that are inherent to the approaches used for separation of samples for mass spectrometry analysis. So, while reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC) affords excellent retention of lipophilic molecules, it does not retain highly polar species, while hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography (HILIC) is a reversal of this selectivity.

To address the limitations, an integrated workflow that integrates both RPLC/MS and HILIC/MS analyses from the same metabolic extract was established. An aminopropyl-based HILIC method was optimized with various salts and pHs and was applied to perturbed bacterial metabolomes. Using this optimized HILIC approach, metabolites from central carbon metabolic pathways such as amino acids, organic acids, and phosphorylated sugars that were not detectable in RPLC eluted in the void volume of the column were successfully identified.

Proof of principle was obtained by using bacteria, human plasma and mouse cancer cells as biological samples. The same extracts (80% MeOH), prepared by a single common procedure, afforded mass spectra containing more than 30,000 features, derived from lipids and central carbon metabolism, for all three biological samples. A detailed evaluation showed that, for bacteria and human plasma, the largest number of unique features was provided by RPLC in the ESI+ mode, whereas the largest number of unique features derived from HILIC was obtained in the ESI- mode. Therefore, when samples are limited in amount, time is a crucial variable and, in the conditions here considered, the best possible “metabolic snapshot” can be afforded by combining RPLC/ESI+ with HILIC/ESI-.
